operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Unit Testing (151ecba898d7ad36)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Unit Testing
Messages: 3
Date range: 2015-12-29 to 2015-12-29
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 151ecba898d7ad36

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Mark Nuzz
Date: 2015-12-29

I didn't see a Wiki page for the unit testing, and I've never run unit tests on a Cxx project. If anyone could point me in the right direction.

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-12-29

Its easy: mkdir build; cd build; cmake ..; make; make test. Thats it. On a multi-core machine try make -jN where N is the number of cpu cores.

--linas

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Misgana Bayetta
Date: 2015-12-29

While you are in the build dir: make -j$(nproc) CogserverUTest, cd tests, ctest CogserverUTest -V. You can query available targets by typing make tab tab.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Unordered Set in AtomTable (14c1501fb1890282)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Unordered Set in AtomTable
Messages: 3
Date range: 13 Mar 2015 to 2015-03-13
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14c1501fb1890282

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Curtis M. Faith
Date: 13 Mar 2015

In looking deeper at the AtomSpace internals, I noticed that AtomTable holds a std::set of Handles rather than std::unordered_set. Changing AtomTable to use std:unordered_set changes the resolution time from UUID to AtomPtr significantly. Resolve time drops from 0.667us to 0.312us at 1M nodes.

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-03-13

well, I committed the change; however, your results are contradicted by typical industry benchmarks, which show that set is anywhere from 4x to 12x faster than unordered set for most compilers. I cannot really think of a plausible explanation for what you are seeing.

-- Linas

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-03-13

argh. Raw ticks. I swear, I want to wring the guys neck. What was he thinking? Who does stuff like that?

--linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Unsupervised learning (14b7c61a5da90079)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Unsupervised learning
Messages: 4
Date range: 2015-02-12 to 2015-02-12
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14b7c61a5da90079

============================================================

--- Message 1/4 ---
From: Aaron Nitzkin
Date: 2015-02-12

So, I've managed to find some software tools that generate n-gram lists, with various statistics including transition probabilities between words, and tools that generate lists of collocates with MI. [...]

--- Message 2/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-02-12

Hi,

I am mildly concerned that we have two incompatible and competing approaches to language learning, but I guess competition is OK.

Transition probabilities are probabilities. MI is a complex formula involving logarithms and division. Typical text corpus MI range from +30 to -10 and anything under +4 is junk.

Markov chains are a special case of link-grammar. It is well known that natural language is not describable by regexes or finite state machines; you need at least a context-free language. That's why LG was invented.

-- Linas

--- Message 3/4 ---
From: Aaron Nitzkin
Date: 2015-02-13

Impossible. I am completely on board with you as far as the strategy you have outlined. I was merely reporting on some things that popped up when playing around with some software tools.

--- Message 4/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-02-12

Probabilities are multiplicative, MI is additive. For LG, it is nicer to say the total cost of this parse is the sum of the costs of the links.

I've been planning on writing down the N=3 and N=4 cases formally for the last year but haven't done so. I have never seen it written down anywhere. You have to specify the different relations that 3 or 4 things can have with one-another.

--linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+URE and chaining was Re OpenCog unit tests debugging (14f091b459ac454f)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: URE and chaining was Re OpenCog unit tests debugging
Messages: 3
Date range: 2015-08-07 to 2015-08-13
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14f091b459ac454f

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-08-07

Attached is a short note about rule chaining. I thought I was going to write something much longer, but I promptly got lost in the weeds. There are a bunch of issues, and its hard to even talk about the later ones until the earlier ones get resolved.

--linas

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-08-08

Linas, Regarding the representation of contextual/hypothetical truth values, I thought we had settled on the mechanism you suggested as in the example at http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Claims_and_contexts

This seemed to give a reasonably simple way of saying 'The proposition that Atom X has TruthValue T' without actually assigning Atom X TruthValue T in the Atomspace.

-- Ben

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-08-13

Hi Linas, I think you're right -- something like this probably does need to be added into the URE at some point, and we have not yet made concrete plans to do so, because of the folks working on URE/PLN being busy struggling with other issues.

I will think about it more.

ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Using a Docker container for development (14b2e353dc378e5c)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Using a Docker container for development
Messages: 3
Date range: 2015-01-28 to 28 Jan 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14b2e353dc378e5c

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-01-28

Jim Rutt and I were talking about using a Docker container for development (not just deployment). Ideally, OpenCog sits in a Docker container while the IDE sits outside. Using the IDE, one can compile/build and run a debugger on the code in the Docker container.

-- Ben

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Joel Pitt
Date: 2015-01-29

I would suggest breaking OpenCog into component containers: a relex container, a cogserver container, other embodiment server containers. Basically, if something exposes a network port, it'd be worth thinking about if it should be its own container.

You may even be able to attach to the opencog process (inside the docker container) directly from the host without remote debugging.

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 28 Jan 2015

Hi Joel, some of your suggestions have already been implemented. Check https://github.com/opencog/opencog/tree/master/docker. Of course there is room for improvement.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Using Embodiment Proxy to connect a physical robot (14c13514d8d6da1a)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Using Embodiment Proxy to connect a physical robot
Messages: 2
Date range: 13 Mar 2015 to 2015-03-13
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14c13514d8d6da1a

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Sean Sekora
Date: 13 Mar 2015

I'm just starting to get my feet wet with OpenCog so please excuse me if I'm asking a dumb question.

Is it possible to use the Embodiment Proxy to connect a physical robot?

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-03-13

I'm pretty sure that the answer is "no". The current ideas, and status for connecting to a real-world robot is described here:
http://wiki.opencog.org/w/New_Embodiment_Module,_March_2015

--linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Using MOSES to learn the parameters of a neural network (150f44ea00bfd32d)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Using MOSES to learn the parameters of a neural network
Messages: 8
Date range: 2015-11-10 to 2015-11-15
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 150f44ea00bfd32d

============================================================

--- Message 1/8 ---
From: Cosmo Harrigan
Date: 2015-11-10

How well should MOSES work for learning the parameters of a neural network (neuroevolution)? For instance, at AGI 2015, Schmidhuber talked about their work at IDSIA using genetic algorithms for choosing the parameters of a convolutional neural network. I found this page from 2009 that discusses a similar application of MOSES but I am curious what a more current assessment would be?

--- Message 2/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-11

Historically, MOSES has not extremely good at optimizing floating-point values... However, in his GSoC project in 2015, Arley Ristar implemented PSO as an alternative algorithm for intra-deme search in MOSES for the floating point case. And I note that PSO is a viable approach to training NNs, perhaps better than backprop. Also, MOSES clearly *is* a good approach for learning the *structure* of neural nets. So I would say that: getting the MOSES w/ PSO inside to work effectively as an algorithm for learning NNs structures plus parameters is a viable research project, but would likely require a fair bit of tweaking and tuning...

--- Message 3/8 ---
From: Cosmo Harrigan
Date: 2015-11-10

Thanks, thats an interesting direction to read more about. Maybe one possible step in that direction could be to modify the Pole Balancing code that Joel Lehman wrote to use the PSO algorithm?

--- Message 4/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-11

Yes...

--- Message 5/8 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-11-11

Yes. But we probably need some simpler PSO examples, as well. e.g. learning a single linear classifying hyperplane. A more impressive demo would be one where moses learns two or three hyperplanes to carve up some space into quadrants or octants. The linear kernels cant learn these at all, and most other algos kind-of suck at this, although NNs can do this well. However, moses +PSO should be able to learn these quickly, without using/being/emulating a neural net.

--- Message 6/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-15

Cosmo, Regarding the pole balancing example, if you havent seen it please read the GSoC 2009 wiki page on Extending MOSES to evolve Recurrent Neural Networks which explains the many interesting complexities here...

--- Message 7/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-15

Oh, never mind, I see you already found that page...

--- Message 8/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-15

Pretty much, where he left off, we were unsure if the crappy results were due to a bad optimizer or some subtler phenomenon. Trying with PSO would help resolve that...

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+where is MOSES (14f8f4bad29b0a76)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: where is MOSES
Messages: 4
Date range: 2 Sep 2015 to 3 Sep 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14f8f4bad29b0a76

============================================================

--- Message 1/4 ---
From: theiman
Date: 2 Sep 2015

Hi Y'all, I installed OPENCOG but there is no MOSES. Did I do something wrong? I am trying to build an R gui for MOSES (I am working with Mike Duncan).

--- Message 2/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-09-02

moses is in https://github.com/opencog/moses

We split it out from the rest of opencog a while ago. The Noobs instructions need to be updated. Perhaps you could do this?

--linas

--- Message 3/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-09-02

It is built and installed just like the other parts are. To build moses, you need to have previously gotten cogutils. Moses does not prereq the atomspace or opencog. The README is pretty much guaranteed to be more accurate than the wiki.

--linas

--- Message 4/4 ---
From: theiman
Date: 3 Sep 2015

Hi Linas, Thank you!! That was pretty easy. I will document what I did and update the appropriate Noob section.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Where to find the list of programs that should be built by the build (14b112393bfba3c1)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Where to find the list of programs that should be built by the build
Messages: 5
Date range: 22 Jan 2015 to 2015-01-25
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14b112393bfba3c1

============================================================

--- Message 1/5 ---
From: olegsivokon@gmail.com
Date: 22 Jan 2015

Preferably with some kind of description of what they are used for.



I've tried reading the Wiki, but it assumes too much knowledge of OpenCog 

structure and it's parts and too little knowledge of everything else, so 

it's easy to miss things, even if they are there.



Here, to explain my confu

--- Message 2/5 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 22 Jan 2015

This my not address your enquiry, but to get up and running with opencog on other linux distros, it would be best to use docker. Check http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Building_OpenCog

--- Message 3/5 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-01-22

RHEL shouldn't be all that different. Once you install dependencies, it

should be the same.



For opencog, there is only one executable: opencog/server/cogserver



For moses, there's a half-dozen, but really only 1 or 2 that matter.

 That's pretty much it. I can't think of anything else



--linas



On Thu, Jan 22, 2015 at 8:12 AM, Amen Belayneh <amenbelayneh@gmail.com>

wrote:

--- Message 4/5 ---
From: olegsivokon@gmail.com
Date: 25 Jan 2015

after the compilation succeeds this file isn't produced.  I also couldn't 

find the Makefile which would generate it. Will it help if I post the 

output of the Make / the compilation log?



Best,



Oleg

--- Message 5/5 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-01-25

after cmake runs, it prints a list of the things that will be built.

If cogserver is not listed, then one of the dependencies is missing.

Review tehse dependencies.



--linas
